title: Whittled birch letter opener
subtitle: A slender flat-bladed letter opener in seasoned birch
excerpt: A slender birch letter opener with a flat blade, a defined shoulder, and a comfortable rounded handle. About 22 cm finished length. A single-knife project that builds push-cut precision and teaches controlled taper work.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics for single-knife carving method. Letter openers documented in late-Victorian domestic craft manuals and in the Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on treen and desk accessories.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content (8 to 12 percent for indoor use). Dimensionally stable; joints and proportions will not move after fitting.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.

content.text: A letter opener is a good project for
seasoned wood

because the thin blade section needs dimensional stability. Green wood would dry and check (develop hairline cracks) along the thin blade. Birch is the choice here: fine-grained, pale, easy to work with a sharp
sloyd knife
. A piece of straight-grained seasoned birch 24 cm long, 30 mm wide, and 15 mm thick is the starting material.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Birch takes a clean finish, holds a thin section well, and works well with a sloyd knife. For seasoned stock, look for timber merchants, craft-wood suppliers, or offcuts from a joinery workshop. The moisture content should be 8 to 12 percent for indoor work; a moisture meter confirms this.
Cherry is a good alternative: darker colour, similar working properties. Lime (basswood) is easier to carve but softer and less durable.
Shaping
Draw the outline on the flat face: a blade 13 cm long tapering from 22 mm at the shoulder to 8 mm at the tip, and a handle 11 cm with a slight taper down to a rounded pommel.

Cut the blade taper first with push cuts. Seasoned birch is harder than green wood; take thinner shavings and let the edge do more work. The cuts should produce thin, curling shavings, not thick crumbling chips. If the shavings crumble, the edge needs sharpening.

The shoulder between blade and handle is the precise boundary. Use a stop cut across the full width of the shoulder first, then pare from the blade section up to that stop cut. This prevents the shaping cuts from running past the shoulder into the handle.

Reduce the blade thickness from 15 mm to 3 mm at the shoulder and 1.5 mm at the tip. Work both faces evenly so the blade stays centred. The blade needs to be stiff enough not to flex in use; 3 mm in birch is about right.
Handle and finish

Round the handle to an oval cross-section with push cuts. Sand with 400 grit if a polished surface is wanted. Apply Danish oil: three thin coats with a light sand between coats two and three. Danish oil sets quickly and builds a soft satin finish on birch.
Variations
Tapered octagonal handle: instead of rounding to an oval, stop at the eight-sided stage for a more traditional feel.
Cherry version: darker grain, similar difficulty. Cherry turns a warm red-brown with age and Danish oil.

rows.cause: Blade thinned too quickly in one pass, leaving a stress point
rows.fix: Thin both faces in small steps, alternating between faces. Never take more than 1 mm per pass near the 3 mm target thickness.

rows.symptom: Tip finished too narrow and fragile
rows.cause: Taper went past 1 mm at the tip
rows.fix: Widen the tip slightly with light slicing cuts along the edge. The letter opener tip only needs to slip under a paper flap, not pierce anything.
